claim.content:
Consider a streaming-data setting where the observed interaction history up to time t is X_{1:t}=\{x_1,\dots,x_t\} and the agent's memory state at time t is the random variable M_t. Define the Information Bottleneck Lagrangian for a memory construction policy as \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{IB}}(M_t)=I(X_{1:t};M_t)-\beta I(M_t;Y), where I(\cdot;\cdot) denotes mutual information, Y denotes latent future tasks, and \beta>0 controls the compression–relevance trade-off. Because the combinatorial space of possible memory configurations across a long horizon is intractably large, a practical online strategy is to make locally greedy, time-step decisions that minimize the incremental cost
      $$
      \Delta\mathcal{L}=\bigl[I(X_{1:t+1};M_{t+1})-I(X_{1:t};M_t)\bigr]-\beta\bigl[I(M_{t+1};Y)-I(M_t;Y)\bigr],
      $$
      i.e., to select merges/links/appends at each ingestion step that locally reduce \Delta\mathcal{L}. The proposition is that this greedy, stepwise minimization yields a tractable approximation whose resulting memory trajectory attains substantially lower overall \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{IB}} than naive baselines in practice and therefore serves as an acceptable proxy for the intractable global optimization over the full sequence of memory states M_{1:T}, under the additional modeling assumption that early local consolidation decisions rarely induce large, adversarial long-range negative interactions (formally, that large negative second-order effects from early merges are uncommon).
